name: distlib

documentation:
---
substitutions:

    ```{image} https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/pypa/distlib/package-tests.yml
    :alt: GitHub Workflow Status (with event)
    ```
  badge2: |-
    ```{image} https://img.shields.io/codecov/c/github/pypa/distlib
    :alt: GitHub coverage status
    :target: https://app.codecov.io/gh/pypa/distlib

{{ badge1 }} {{ badge2 }}

# What is it?

Distlib is a library which implements low-level functions that relate to
packaging and distribution of Python software. It is intended to be used as the
basis for third-party packaging tools. The documentation is available at

<https://distlib.readthedocs.io/>

# Main features

Distlib currently offers the following features:

- The package `distlib.database`, which implements a database of installed
  distributions, as defined by {pep}`376`, and distribution dependency graph
  logic. Support is also provided for non-installed distributions (i.e.
  distributions registered with metadata on an index like PyPI), including
  the ability to scan for dependencies and building dependency graphs.
- The package `distlib.index`, which implements an interface to perform
  operations on an index, such as registering a project, uploading a
  distribution or uploading documentation. Support is included for verifying
  SSL connections (with domain matching) and signing/verifying packages using
  GnuPG.
- The package `distlib.metadata`, which implements distribution metadata as
  defined by {pep}`643`, {pep}`566`, {pep}`345`, {pep}`314` and {pep}`241`.
- The package `distlib.markers`, which implements environment markers as
  defined by {pep}`508`.
- The package `distlib.manifest`, which implements lists of files used
  in packaging source distributions.
- The package `distlib.locators`, which allows finding distributions, whether
  on PyPI (XML-RPC or via the "simple" interface), local directories or some
  other source.
- The package `distlib.resources`, which allows access to data files stored
  in Python packages, both in the file system and in .zip files.
- The package `distlib.scripts`, which allows installing of scripts with
  adjustment of shebang lines and support for native Windows executable
  launchers.
- The package `distlib.version`, which implements version specifiers as
  defined by {pep}`440`, but also support for working with "legacy" versions and
  semantic versions.
- The package `distlib.wheel`, which provides support for building and
  installing from the Wheel format for binary distributions (see {pep}`427`).
- The package `distlib.util`, which contains miscellaneous functions and
  classes which are useful in packaging, but which do not fit neatly into
  one of the other packages in `distlib`.\* The package implements enhanced
  globbing functionality such as the ability to use `**` in patterns to
  specify recursing into subdirectories.

# Python version and platform compatibility

Distlib is intended to be used on and is tested on Python versions 2.7 and 3.6 or later,
pypy-2.7 and pypy3 on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

# Project status

The project has reached a mature status in its development: there is a comprehensive
test suite and it has been exercised on Windows, Ubuntu and macOS. The project is used
by well-known projects such as [pip](https://pypi.org/pypi/pip) and [caniusepython3](https://pypi.org/pypi/caniusepython3).

This project was migrated from Mercurial to Git and from BitBucket to GitHub, and
although all information of importance has been retained across the migration, some
commit references in issues and issue comments may have become invalid.

# Code of Conduct

Everyone interacting in the distlib project's codebases, issue trackers, chat
rooms, and mailing lists is expected to follow the [PyPA Code of Conduct].

[pypa code of conduct]: https://www.pypa.io/en/latest/code-of-conduct/

classes.name: distlib.scripts.ScriptMaker

methods.name: distlib.scripts.ScriptMaker._is_shell
documentation:
Determine if the specified executable is a script
(contains a #! line)

methods.name: distlib.scripts.ScriptMaker._build_shebang
documentation:
Build a shebang line. In the simple case (on Windows, or a shebang line
which is not too long or contains spaces) use a simple formulation for
the shebang. Otherwise, use /bin/sh as the executable, with a contrived
shebang which allows the script to run either under Python or sh, using
suitable quoting. Thanks to Harald Nordgren for his input.

See also: http://www.in-ulm.de/~mascheck/various/shebang/#length
          https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/tip/mach

methods.name: distlib.scripts.ScriptMaker._get_launcher
documentation: Executable launcher support.
Launchers are from https://bitbucket.org/vinay.sajip/simple_launcher/

methods.name: distlib.scripts.ScriptMaker.make
documentation:
Make a script.

```
param specification

The specification, which is either a valid export
entry specification (to make a script from a
callable) or a filename (to make a script by
copying from a source location).

param options

A dictionary of options controlling script generation.

return

A list of all absolute pathnames written to.
```
Public API follows

documentation:
Process a directive which either adds some files from `allfiles` to

```
param directive

The directive to process. This should be in a format
compatible with distutils MANIFEST.in files:

http://docs.python.org/distutils/sourcedist.html#commands
```

methods.name: distlib.manifest.Manifest._parse_directive
documentation:
Validate a directive.
\:param directive: The directive to validate.
\:return: A tuple of action, patterns, thedir, dir_patterns
Private API

methods.name: distlib.manifest.Manifest._include_pattern
documentation:
Select strings (presumably filenames) from 'self.files' that
match 'pattern', a Unix-style wildcard (glob) pattern.

Patterns are not quite the same as implemented by the 'fnmatch'
module: '*' and '?'  match non-special characters, where "special"
is platform-dependent: slash on Unix; colon, slash, and backslash on
DOS/Windows; and colon on Mac OS.

If 'anchor' is true (the default), then the pattern match is more
stringent: "*.py" will match "foo.py" but not "foo/bar.py".  If
'anchor' is false, both of these will match.

If 'prefix' is supplied, then only filenames starting with 'prefix'
(itself a pattern) and ending with 'pattern', with anything in between
them, will match.  'anchor' is ignored in this case.

If 'is_regex' is true, 'anchor' and 'prefix' are ignored, and
'pattern' is assumed to be either a string containing a regex or a
regex object -- no translation is done, the regex is just compiled
and used as-is.

Selected strings will be added to self.files.

Return True if files are found.

methods.name: distlib.manifest.Manifest._exclude_pattern
documentation:
Remove strings (presumably filenames) from 'files' that match
'pattern'.

Other parameters are the same as for 'include_pattern()', above.
The list 'self.files' is modified in place. Return True if files are
found.

This API is public to allow e.g. exclusion of SCM subdirs, e.g. when
packaging source distributions

methods.name: distlib.manifest.Manifest._translate_pattern
documentation:
Translate a shell-like wildcard pattern to a compiled regular
expression.

Return the compiled regex.  If 'is_regex' true,
then 'pattern' is directly compiled to a regex (if it's a string)
or just returned as-is (assumes it's a regex object).

methods.name: distlib.manifest.Manifest._glob_to_re
documentation:
Translate a shell-like glob pattern to a regular expression.

Return a string containing the regex.  Differs from
'fnmatch.translate()' in that '*' does not match "special characters"
(which are platform-specific).

A list of files built by exploring the filesystem and filtered by applying various
patterns to what we find there.

modules.name: distlib.version
documentation:
Implementation of a flexible versioning scheme providing support for PEP-440,
setuptools-compatible and semantic versioning.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (C) 2012-2023 The Python Software Foundation.
See LICENSE.txt and CONTRIBUTORS.txt.

functions.name: distlib.version._suggest_semantic_version
documentation:
Try to suggest a semantic form for a version for which
_suggest_normalized_version couldn't come up with anything.

functions.name: distlib.version._suggest_normalized_version
documentation:
Suggest a normalized version close to the given version string.

If you have a version string that isn't rational (i.e. NormalizedVersion
doesn't like it) then you might be able to get an equivalent (or close)
rational version from this function.

This does a number of simple normalizations to the given string, based
on observation of versions currently in use on PyPI. Given a dump of
those version during PyCon 2009, 4287 of them:
- 2312 (53.93%) match NormalizedVersion without change
  with the automatic suggestion
- 3474 (81.04%) match when using this suggestion method

@param s {str} An irrational version string.
@returns A rational version string, or None, if couldn't determine one.

classes.name: distlib.version.UnsupportedVersionError
documentation: This is an unsupported version.

methods.name: distlib.version.Version.__hash__
documentation: See http://docs.python.org/reference/datamodel#object.__hash__

methods.name: distlib.version.Matcher.parse_requirement
documentation: this is a method only to support alternative implementations
via overriding

methods.name: distlib.version.Matcher.match
documentation:
Check if the provided version matches the constraints.

```
param version

The version to match against this instance.

type version

String or  instance.
```

methods.name: distlib.version.Matcher.__hash__
documentation: See http://docs.python.org/reference/datamodel#object.__hash__

classes.name: distlib.version.NormalizedVersion
documentation:
A rational version.

Good:
    1.2         # equivalent to "1.2.0"
    1.2.0
    1.2a1
    1.2.3a2
    1.2.3b1
    1.2.3c1
    1.2.3.4
    TODO: fill this out

Bad:
    1           # minimum two numbers
    1.2a        # release level must have a release serial
    1.2.3b

methods.name: distlib.version.VersionScheme.is_valid_constraint_list
documentation: Used for processing some metadata fields

See LICENSE.txt and CONTRIBUTORS.txt.

functions.name: distlib.compat._dnsname_match
documentation:
Matching according to RFC 6125, section 6.4.3

http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6125#section-6.4.3

functions.name: distlib.compat.match_hostname
documentation:
Verify that *cert* (in decoded format as returned by
SSLSocket.getpeercert()) matches the *hostname*.  RFC 2818 and RFC 6125
rules are followed, but IP addresses are not accepted for *hostname*.

CertificateError is raised on failure. On success, the function
returns nothing.

functions.name: distlib.compat.which
documentation:
Given a command, mode, and a PATH string, return the path which
conforms to the given mode on the PATH, or None if there is no such
file.

`mode` defaults to os.F_OK | os.X_OK. `path` defaults to the result
of os.environ.get("PATH"), or can be overridden with a custom search
path.
Implementation from Python 3.3

functions.name: distlib.compat.python_implementation
documentation: Return a string identifying the Python implementation.

functions.name: distlib.compat._get_normal_name
documentation: Imitates get_normal_name in tokenizer.c.

functions.name: distlib.compat.detect_encoding
documentation:
The detect_encoding() function is used to detect the encoding that should
be used to decode a Python source file.  It requires one argument, readline,
in the same way as the tokenize() generator.

It will call readline a maximum of twice, and return the encoding used
(as a string) and a list of any lines (left as bytes) it has read in.

It detects the encoding from the presence of a utf-8 bom or an encoding
cookie as specified in pep-0263.  If both a bom and a cookie are present,
but disagree, a SyntaxError will be raised.  If the encoding cookie is an
invalid charset, raise a SyntaxError.  Note that if a utf-8 bom is found,
'utf-8-sig' is returned.

If no encoding is specified, then the default of 'utf-8' will be returned.

functions.name: distlib.compat._recursive_repr
documentation:
Decorator to make a repr function return fillvalue for a recursive
call

classes.name: distlib.compat.Container
documentation: A generic container for when multiple values need to be returned

classes.name: distlib.compat.ChainMap

methods.name: distlib.compat.ChainMap.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a ChainMap by setting *maps* to the given mappings.
If no mappings are provided, a single empty dictionary is used.

A ChainMap groups multiple dicts (or other mappings) together
to create a single, updateable view.

The underlying mappings are stored in a list.  That list is public and can
accessed or updated using the *maps* attribute.  There is no other state.

Lookups search the underlying mappings successively until a key is found.
In contrast, writes, updates, and deletions only operate on the first
mapping.

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.__init__
documentation:
Initialize an ordered dictionary.  Signature is the same as for
regular dictionaries, but keyword arguments are not recommended
because their insertion order is arbitrary.
An inherited dict maps keys to values.
The inherited dict provides __getitem__, __len__, __contains__, and get.
The remaining methods are order-aware.
Big-O running times for all methods are the same as for regular dictionaries.
The internal self.__map dictionary maps keys to links in a doubly linked list.
The circular doubly linked list starts and ends with a sentinel element.
The sentinel element never gets deleted (this simplifies the algorithm).
Each link is stored as a list of length three:  [PREV, NEXT, KEY].

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.popitem
documentation:
od.popitem() -> (k, v), return and remove a (key, value) pair.
Pairs are returned in LIFO order if last is true or FIFO order if false.

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.keys
documentation: -- the following methods do not depend on the internal structure --

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.update
documentation:
od.update(E, **F) -> None.  Update od from dict/iterable E and F.

If E is a dict instance, does:           for k in E: od[k] = E[k]
If E has a .keys() method, does:         for k in E.keys(): od[k] = E[k]
Or if E is an iterable of items, does:   for k, v in E: od[k] = v
In either case, this is followed by:     for k, v in F.items(): od[k] = v

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.pop
documentation:
od.pop(k[,d]) -> v, remove specified key and return the corresponding value.
If key is not found, d is returned if given, otherwise KeyError is raised.

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.fromkeys
documentation:
OD.fromkeys(S[, v]) -> New ordered dictionary with keys from S
and values equal to v (which defaults to None).

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.__eq__
documentation:
od.__eq__(y) <==> od==y.  Comparison to another OD is order-sensitive
while comparison to a regular mapping is order-insensitive.

methods.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.viewkeys
documentation: -- the following methods are only used in Python 2.7 --

class_variables.name: distlib.compat.OrderedDict.__update
documentation: let subclasses override update without breaking __init__

classes.name: distlib.compat.ConvertingDict
documentation: A converting dictionary wrapper.
The ConvertingXXX classes are wrappers around standard Python containers,
and they serve to convert any suitable values in the container. The
conversion converts base dicts, lists and tuples to their wrapped
equivalents, whereas strings which match a conversion format are converted
appropriately.
Each wrapper should have a configurator attribute holding the actual
configurator to use for conversion.

classes.name: distlib.compat.ConvertingList
documentation: A converting list wrapper.

classes.name: distlib.compat.ConvertingTuple
documentation: A converting tuple wrapper.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.resolve
documentation:
Resolve strings to objects using standard import and attribute
syntax.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.ext_convert
documentation: Default converter for the ext:// protocol.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.cfg_convert
documentation: Default converter for the cfg:// protocol.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.convert
documentation:
Convert values to an appropriate type. dicts, lists and tuples are
replaced by their converting alternatives. Strings are checked to
see if they have a conversion format and are converted if they do.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.configure_custom
documentation: Configure an object with a user-supplied factory.

methods.name: distlib.compat.BaseConfigurator.as_tuple
documentation: Utility function which converts lists to tuples.
The configurator base class which defines some useful defaults.

Return the distribution name with version.

If filesafe is true, return a filename-escaped form.
Public API

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.is_field
documentation: return True if name is a valid metadata key

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.read
documentation: Read the metadata values from a file path.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.read_file
documentation: Read the metadata values from a file object.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.write
documentation: Write the metadata fields to filepath.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.write_file
documentation: Write the PKG-INFO format data to a file object.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.update
documentation:
Set metadata values from the given iterable `other` and kwargs.

Behavior is like `dict.update`: If `other` has a `keys` method,
they are looped over and `self[key]` is assigned `other[key]`.
Else, `other` is an iterable of `(key, value)` iterables.

Keys that don't match a metadata field or that have an empty value are
dropped.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.set
documentation: Control then set a metadata field.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.get
documentation: Get a metadata field.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.check
documentation:
Check if the metadata is compliant. If strict is True then raise if
no Name or Version are provided

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.todict
documentation:
Return fields as a dict.

Field names will be converted to use the underscore-lowercase style
instead of hyphen-mixed case (i.e. home_page instead of Home-page).
This is as per https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0566/#id17.

methods.name: distlib.metadata.LegacyMetadata.keys
documentation: Mapping API
TODO could add iter* variants

The legacy metadata of a release.

Supports versions 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 2.0 and 1.3/2.1 (auto-detected). You can
instantiate the class with one of these arguments (or none):
- *path*, the path to a metadata file
- *fileobj* give a file-like object with metadata as content
- *mapping* is a dict-like object
- *scheme* is a version scheme name

classes.name: distlib.metadata.Metadata

methods.name: distlib.metadata.Metadata.get_requirements
documentation:
Base method to get dependencies, given a set of extras
to satisfy and an optional environment context.
\:param reqts: A list of sometimes-wanted dependencies,

> perhaps dependent on extras and environment.

```
param extras

A list of optional components being requested.

param env

An optional environment for marker evaluation.
```

The metadata of a release. This implementation uses 2.1
metadata where possible. If not possible, it wraps a LegacyMetadata
instance which handles the key-value metadata format.

Get the distutils command for interacting with PyPI configurations.
\:return: the command.

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.read_configuration

Read the PyPI access configuration as supported by distutils. This populates
`username`, `password`, `realm` and `url` attributes from the
configuration.

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.save_configuration

Save the PyPI access configuration. You must have set `username` and
`password` attributes before calling this method.

documentation:
Register a distribution on PyPI, using the provided metadata.

```
param metadata

A  instance defining at least a name
and version number for the distribution to be
registered.

return

The HTTP response received from PyPI upon submission of the
request.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex._reader
documentation:
Thread runner for reading lines of from a subprocess into a buffer.

```
param name

The logical name of the stream (used for logging only).

param stream

The stream to read from. This will typically a pipe
connected to the output stream of a subprocess.

param outbuf

The list to append the read lines to.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.get_sign_command
documentation:
Return a suitable command for signing a file.

```
param filename

The pathname to the file to be signed.

param signer

The identifier of the signer of the file.

param sign_password

The passphrase for the signer's
private key used for signing.

param keystore

The path to a directory which contains the keys
used in verification. If not specified, the
instance's gpg_home attribute is used instead.

return

The signing command as a list suitable to be
passed to .
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.run_command
documentation:
Run a command in a child process , passing it any input data specified.

```
param cmd

The command to run.

param input_data

If specified, this must be a byte string containing
data to be sent to the child process.

return

A tuple consisting of the subprocess' exit code, a list of
lines read from the subprocess' stdout, and a list of
lines read from the subprocess' stderr.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.sign_file
documentation:
Sign a file.

```
param filename

The pathname to the file to be signed.

param signer

The identifier of the signer of the file.

param sign_password

The passphrase for the signer's
private key used for signing.

param keystore

The path to a directory which contains the keys
used in signing. If not specified, the instance's
gpg_home attribute is used instead.

return

The absolute pathname of the file where the signature is
stored.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.upload_file
documentation:
Upload a release file to the index.

```
param metadata

A  instance defining at least a name
and version number for the file to be uploaded.

param filename

The pathname of the file to be uploaded.

param signer

The identifier of the signer of the file.

param sign_password

The passphrase for the signer's
private key used for signing.

param filetype

The type of the file being uploaded. This is the
distutils command which produced that file, e.g.
sdist or bdist_wheel.

param pyversion

The version of Python which the release relates
to. For code compatible with any Python, this would
be source, otherwise it would be e.g. 3.2.

param keystore

The path to a directory which contains the keys
used in signing. If not specified, the instance's
gpg_home attribute is used instead.

return

The HTTP response received from PyPI upon submission of the
request.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.upload_documentation
documentation:
Upload documentation to the index.

```
param metadata

A  instance defining at least a name
and version number for the documentation to be
uploaded.

param doc_dir

The pathname of the directory which contains the
documentation. This should be the directory that
contains the index.html for the documentation.

return

The HTTP response received from PyPI upon submission of the
request.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.get_verify_command
documentation:
Return a suitable command for verifying a file.

```
param signature_filename

The pathname to the file containing the
signature.

param data_filename

The pathname to the file containing the
signed data.

param keystore

The path to a directory which contains the keys
used in verification. If not specified, the
instance's gpg_home attribute is used instead.

return

The verifying command as a list suitable to be
passed to .
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.verify_signature
documentation:
Verify a signature for a file.

```
param signature_filename

The pathname to the file containing the
signature.

param data_filename

The pathname to the file containing the
signed data.

param keystore

The path to a directory which contains the keys
used in verification. If not specified, the
instance's gpg_home attribute is used instead.

return

True if the signature was verified, else False.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.download_file
documentation:
This is a convenience method for downloading a file from an URL.
Normally, this will be a file from the index, though currently
no check is made for this (i.e. a file can be downloaded from
anywhere).

The method is just like the {func}`urlretrieve` function in the
standard library, except that it allows digest computation to be
done during download and checking that the downloaded data
matched any expected value.

```
param url

The URL of the file to be downloaded (assumed to be
available via an HTTP GET request).

param destfile

The pathname where the downloaded file is to be
saved.

param digest

If specified, this must be a (hasher, value)
tuple, where hasher is the algorithm used (e.g.
'md5') and value is the expected value.

param reporthook

The same as for  in the
standard library.
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.send_request
documentation:
Send a standard library {class}`Request` to PyPI and return its
response.

```
param req

The request to send.

return

The HTTP response from PyPI (a standard library HTTPResponse).
```

methods.name: distlib.index.PackageIndex.encode_request
documentation:
Encode fields and files for posting to an HTTP server.

```
param fields

The fields to send as a list of (fieldname, value)
tuples.

param files

The files to send as a list of (fieldname, filename,
file_bytes) tuple.
```

This class represents a package index compatible with PyPI, the Python
Package Index.

modules.name: distlib.wheel
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (C) 2013-2023 Vinay Sajip.
Licensed to the Python Software Foundation under a contributor agreement.
See LICENSE.txt and CONTRIBUTORS.txt.

functions.name: distlib.wheel.compatible_tags
documentation: Return (pyver, abi, arch) tuples compatible with this Python.

variables.name: distlib.wheel.cache
documentation: created when needed

classes.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.__init__
documentation: Initialise an instance using a (valid) filename.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.filename
documentation: Build and return a filename from the various components.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.build
documentation:
Build a wheel from files in specified paths, and use any specified tags
when determining the name of the wheel.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.skip_entry
documentation:
Determine whether an archive entry should be skipped when verifying
or installing.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.install
documentation:
Install a wheel to the specified paths. If kwarg `warner` is
specified, it should be a callable, which will be called with two
tuples indicating the wheel version of this software and the wheel
version in the file, if there is a discrepancy in the versions.
This can be used to issue any warnings to raise any exceptions.
If kwarg `lib_only` is True, only the purelib/platlib files are
installed, and the headers, scripts, data and dist-info metadata are
not written. If kwarg `bytecode_hashed_invalidation` is True, written
bytecode will try to use file-hash based invalidation (PEP-552) on
supported interpreter versions (CPython 3.7+).

The return value is a {class}`InstalledDistribution` instance unless
`options.lib_only` is True, in which case the return value is `None`.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.is_compatible
documentation: Determine if a wheel is compatible with the running system.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.is_mountable
documentation: Determine if a wheel is asserted as mountable by its metadata.

methods.name: distlib.wheel.Wheel.update
documentation:
Update the contents of a wheel in a generic way. The modifier should
be a callable which expects a dictionary argument: its keys are
archive-entry paths, and its values are absolute filesystem paths
where the contents the corresponding archive entries can be found. The
modifier is free to change the contents of the files pointed to, add
new entries and remove entries, before returning. This method will
extract the entire contents of the wheel to a temporary location, call
the modifier, and then use the passed (and possibly updated)
dictionary to write a new wheel. If `dest_dir` is specified, the new
wheel is written there -- otherwise, the original wheel is overwritten.

The modifier should return True if it updated the wheel, else False.
This method returns the same value the modifier returns.
Class to build and install from Wheel files (PEP 427).

modules.name: distlib.locators
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (C) 2012-2023 Vinay Sajip.
Licensed to the Python Software Foundation under a contributor agreement.
See LICENSE.txt and CONTRIBUTORS.txt.

functions.name: distlib.locators.get_all_distribution_names
documentation:
Return all distribution names known by an index.
\:param url: The URL of the index.
\:return: A list of all known distribution names.

classes.name: distlib.locators.RedirectHandler

methods.name: distlib.locators.RedirectHandler.http_error_302
documentation: There's a bug in the base version for some 3.2.x
(e.g. 3.2.2 on Ubuntu Oneiric). If a Location header
returns e.g. /abc, it bails because it says the scheme ''
is bogus, when actually it should use the request's
URL for the scheme. See Python issue #13696.
A class to work around a bug in some Python 3.2.x releases.

classes.name: distlib.locators.Locator

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.
\:param scheme: Because locators look for most recent versions, they

> need to know the version scheme to use. This specifies
> the current PEP-recommended scheme - use `'legacy'`
> if you need to support existing distributions on PyPI.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.get_errors
documentation: Return any errors which have occurred.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.clear_errors
documentation: Clear any errors which may have been logged.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator._get_project
documentation:
For a given project, get a dictionary mapping available versions to Distribution
instances.

This should be implemented in subclasses.

If called from a locate() request, self.matcher will be set to a
matcher for the requirement to satisfy, otherwise it will be None.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.get_project
documentation:
For a given project, get a dictionary mapping available versions to Distribution
instances.

This calls _get_project to do all the work, and just implements a caching layer on top.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.score_url
documentation:
Give an url a score which can be used to choose preferred URLs
for a given project release.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.prefer_url
documentation:
Choose one of two URLs where both are candidates for distribution
archives for the same version of a distribution (for example,
.tar.gz vs. zip).

The current implementation favours https:// URLs over http://, archives
from PyPI over those from other locations, wheel compatibility (if a
wheel) and then the archive name.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.split_filename
documentation: Attempt to split a filename in project name, version and Python version.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.convert_url_to_download_info
documentation:
See if a URL is a candidate for a download URL for a project (the URL
has typically been scraped from an HTML page).

If it is, a dictionary is returned with keys "name", "version",
"filename" and "url"; otherwise, None is returned.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator._get_digest
documentation:
Get a digest from a dictionary by looking at a "digests" dictionary
or keys of the form 'algo_digest'.

Returns a 2-tuple (algo, digest) if found, else None. Currently
looks only for SHA256, then MD5.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator._update_version_data
documentation:
Update a result dictionary (the final result from _get_project) with a
dictionary for a specific version, which typically holds information
gleaned from a filename or URL for an archive for the distribution.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Locator.locate
documentation:
Find the most recent distribution which matches the given
requirement.

```
param requirement

A requirement of the form 'foo (1.0)' or perhaps
'foo (>= 1.0, < 2.0, != 1.3)'

param prereleases

If True, allow pre-release versions
to be located. Otherwise, pre-release versions
are not returned.

return

A  instance, or None if no such
distribution could be located.
```
A base class for locators - things that locate distributions.

classes.name: distlib.locators.PyPIRPCLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.PyPIRPCLocator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.

```
param url

The URL to use for XML-RPC.

param kwargs

Passed to the superclass constructor.
```

methods.name: distlib.locators.PyPIRPCLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.

This locator uses XML-RPC to locate distributions. It therefore
cannot be used with simple mirrors (that only mirror file content).

classes.name: distlib.locators.PyPIJSONLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.PyPIJSONLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.

This locator uses PyPI's JSON interface. It's very limited in functionality
and probably not worth using.

classes.name: distlib.locators.Page

methods.name: distlib.locators.Page.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance with the Unicode page contents and the URL they
came from.

methods.name: distlib.locators.Page.links
documentation:
Return the URLs of all the links on a page together with information
about their "rel" attribute, for determining which ones to treat as
downloads and which ones to queue for further scraping.
This class represents a scraped HTML page.

classes.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.
\:param url: The root URL to use for scraping.
\:param timeout: The timeout, in seconds, to be applied to requests.

> This defaults to `None` (no timeout specified).

```
param num_workers

The number of worker threads you want to do I/O,
This defaults to 10.

param kwargs

Passed to the superclass.
```

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._prepare_threads
documentation:
Threads are created only when get_project is called, and terminate
before it returns. They are there primarily to parallelise I/O (i.e.
fetching web pages).

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._wait_threads
documentation:
Tell all the threads to terminate (by sending a sentinel value) and
wait for them to do so.

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._is_platform_dependent
documentation: Does an URL refer to a platform-specific download?

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._process_download
documentation:
See if an URL is a suitable download for a project.

If it is, register information in the result dictionary (for
_get_project) about the specific version it's for.

Note that the return value isn't actually used other than as a boolean
value.

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._should_queue
documentation:
Determine whether a link URL from a referring page and with a
particular "rel" attribute should be queued for scraping.

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator._fetch
documentation:
Get a URL to fetch from the work queue, get the HTML page, examine its
links for download candidates and candidates for further scraping.

This is a handy method to run in a thread.

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator.get_page
documentation:
Get the HTML for an URL, possibly from an in-memory cache.

XXX TODO Note: this cache is never actually cleared. It's assumed that
the data won't get stale over the lifetime of a locator instance (not
necessarily true for the default_locator).

methods.name: distlib.locators.SimpleScrapingLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.

A locator which scrapes HTML pages to locate downloads for a distribution.
This runs multiple threads to do the I/O; performance is at least as good
as pip's PackageFinder, which works in an analogous fashion.

classes.name: distlib.locators.DirectoryLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.DirectoryLocator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.
\:param path: The root of the directory tree to search.
\:param kwargs: Passed to the superclass constructor,

> except for:
> \* recursive - if True (the default), subdirectories are
>
> > recursed into. If False, only the top-level directory
> > is searched,

methods.name: distlib.locators.DirectoryLocator.should_include
documentation:
Should a filename be considered as a candidate for a distribution
archive? As well as the filename, the directory which contains it
is provided, though not used by the current implementation.

methods.name: distlib.locators.DirectoryLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.
This class locates distributions in a directory tree.

classes.name: distlib.locators.JSONLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.JSONLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.

This locator uses special extended metadata (not available on PyPI) and is
the basis of performant dependency resolution in distlib. Other locators
require archive downloads before dependencies can be determined! As you
might imagine, that can be slow.

classes.name: distlib.locators.DistPathLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.DistPathLocator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.

```
param distpath

A  instance to search.
```

This locator finds installed distributions in a path. It can be useful for
adding to an {class}`AggregatingLocator`.

classes.name: distlib.locators.AggregatingLocator

methods.name: distlib.locators.AggregatingLocator.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.

```
param locators

The list of locators to search.

param kwargs

Passed to the superclass constructor,
except for:
* merge - if False (the default), the first successful

source:7: (ERROR/3) Unexpected indentation.

search from any of the locators is returned. If True,
the results from all locators are merged (this can be
slow).
```

methods.name: distlib.locators.AggregatingLocator.get_distribution_names
documentation: Return all the distribution names known to this locator.
This class allows you to chain and/or merge a list of locators.

classes.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance, using the specified locator
to locate distributions.

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.add_distribution
documentation:
Add a distribution to the finder. This will update internal information
about who provides what.
\:param dist: The distribution to add.

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.remove_distribution
documentation:
Remove a distribution from the finder. This will update internal
information about who provides what.
\:param dist: The distribution to remove.

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.get_matcher
documentation:
Get a version matcher for a requirement.
\:param reqt: The requirement
\:type reqt: str
\:return: A version matcher (an instance of

> {class}`distlib.version.Matcher`).

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.find_providers
documentation:
Find the distributions which can fulfill a requirement.

```
param reqt

The requirement.
:type reqt: str

return

A set of distribution which can fulfill the requirement.
```

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.try_to_replace
documentation:
Attempt to replace one provider with another. This is typically used
when resolving dependencies from multiple sources, e.g. A requires
(B >= 1.0) while C requires (B >= 1.1).

For successful replacement, `provider` must meet all the requirements
which `other` fulfills.

```
param provider

The provider we are trying to replace with.

param other

The provider we're trying to replace.

param problems

If False is returned, this will contain what
problems prevented replacement. This is currently
a tuple of the literal string 'cantreplace',
provider, other  and the set of requirements
that provider couldn't fulfill.

return

True if we can replace other with provider, else
False.
```

methods.name: distlib.locators.DependencyFinder.find
documentation:
Find a distribution and all distributions it depends on.

```
param requirement

The requirement specifying the distribution to
find, or a Distribution instance.

param meta_extras

A list of meta extras such as :test:, :build: and
so on.

param prereleases

If True, allow pre-release versions to be
returned - otherwise, don't return prereleases
unless they're all that's available.
```

Return a set of {class}`Distribution` instances and a set of
problems.

The distributions returned should be such that they have the
{attr}`required` attribute set to `True` if they were
from the `requirement` passed to `find()`, and they have the
{attr}`build_time_dependency` attribute set to `True` unless they
are post-installation dependencies of the `requirement`.

The problems should be a tuple consisting of the string
`'unsatisfied'` and the requirement which couldn't be satisfied
by any distribution known to the locator.
Locate dependencies for distributions.

Create an instance from a path, optionally including legacy (distutils/

If True, this instance will look for and return legacy
distributions as well as those based on PEP 376.
```

documentation: Clears the internal cache.

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath._yield_distributions
documentation: Yield .dist-info and/or .egg(-info) distributions.

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath._generate_cache
documentation:
Scan the path for distributions and populate the cache with
those that are found.

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.distinfo_dirname
documentation:
The *name* and *version* parameters are converted into their
filename-escaped form, i.e. any `'-'` characters are replaced
with `'_'` other than the one in `'dist-info'` and the one
separating the name from the version number.

```
parameter name

is converted to a standard distribution name by replacing
any runs of non- alphanumeric characters with a single
'-'.

type name

string

parameter version

is converted to a standard version string. Spaces
become dots, and all other non-alphanumeric characters
(except dots) become dashes, with runs of multiple
dashes condensed to a single dash.

type version

string

returns

directory name

rtype

string
```

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.get_distributions
documentation:
Provides an iterator that looks for distributions and returns
{class}`InstalledDistribution` or
{class}`EggInfoDistribution` instances for each one of them.

```
rtype

iterator of  and
 instances
```

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.get_distribution
documentation:
Looks for a named distribution on the path.

This function only returns the first result found, as no more than one
value is expected. If nothing is found, `None` is returned.

```
rtype

, 
or None
```

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.provides_distribution
documentation:
Iterates over all distributions to find which distributions provide *name*.
If a *version* is provided, it will be used to filter the results.

This function only returns the first result found, since no more than
one values are expected. If the directory is not found, returns `None`.

```
parameter version

a version specifier that indicates the version
required, conforming to the format in PEP-345

type name

string

type version

string
```

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.get_file_path
documentation: Return the path to a resource file.

methods.name: distlib.database.DistributionPath.get_exported_entries
documentation:
Return all of the exported entries in a particular category.

```
param category

The category to search for entries.

param name

If specified, only entries with that name are returned.
```
Represents a set of distributions installed on a path (typically sys.path).

classes.name: distlib.database.Distribution

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.
\:param metadata: The instance of {class}`Metadata` describing this
distribution.

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.source_url
documentation: The source archive download URL for this distribution.

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.name_and_version
documentation: A utility property which displays the name and version in parentheses.

documentation:
A set of distribution names and versions provided by this distribution.

documentation:
Say if this instance matches (fulfills) a requirement.
\:param req: The requirement to match.
\:rtype req: str
\:return: True if it matches, else False.

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.__repr__
documentation: Return a textual representation of this instance,

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.__eq__
documentation:
See if this distribution is the same as another.
\:param other: The distribution to compare with. To be equal to one

> another. distributions must have the same type, name,
> version and source_url.

```
return

True if it is the same, else False.
```

methods.name: distlib.database.Distribution.__hash__
documentation: Compute hash in a way which matches the equality test.

class_variables.name: distlib.database.Distribution.build_time_dependency
documentation:
Set to True if it's known to be only a build-time dependency (i.e.
not needed after installation).

class_variables.name: distlib.database.Distribution.requested
documentation:
A boolean that indicates whether the `REQUESTED` metadata file is
present (in other words, whether the package was installed by user
request or it was installed as a dependency).

class_variables.name: distlib.database.Distribution.download_url
documentation: Backward compatibility

A base class for distributions, whether installed or from indexes.
Either way, it must have some metadata, so that's all that's needed
for construction.

classes.name: distlib.database.BaseInstalledDistribution

methods.name: distlib.database.BaseInstalledDistribution.__init__
documentation:
Initialise an instance.
\:param metadata: An instance of {class}`Metadata` which describes the

> distribution. This will normally have been initialised
> from a metadata file in the `path`.

```
param path

The path of the .dist-info or .egg-info
directory for the distribution.

param env

This is normally the 
instance where this distribution was found.
```

methods.name: distlib.database.BaseInstalledDistribution.get_hash
documentation:
Get the hash of some data, using a particular hash algorithm, if
specified.

```
param data

The data to be hashed.

type data

bytes

param hasher

The name of a hash implementation, supported by hashlib,
or None. Examples of valid values are 'sha1',
'sha224', 'sha384', 'sha256', 'md5' and
'sha512'. If no hasher is specified, the hasher
attribute of the  instance
is used. If the hasher is determined to be None, MD5
is used as the hashing algorithm.

returns

The hash of the data. If a hasher was explicitly specified,
the returned hash will be prefixed with the specified hasher
followed by '='.

rtype

str
```

This is the base class for installed distributions (whether PEP 376 or
legacy).

classes.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution

Get the list of installed files for the distribution

> as stored in the file (which is as in PEP 376).

documentation:
NOTE: This API may change in the future.

Return the absolute path to a resource file with the given relative
path.

```
param relative_path

The path, relative to .dist-info, of the resource
of interest.

return

The absolute path where the resource is to be found.

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.list_installed_files
documentation:
Iterates over the `RECORD` entries and returns a tuple
`(path, hash, size)` for each line.

```
returns

iterator of (path, hash, size)
```

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.write_installed_files
documentation:
Writes the `RECORD` file, using the `paths` iterable passed in. Any
existing `RECORD` file is silently overwritten.

prefix is used to determine when to write absolute paths.

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.check_installed_files
documentation:
Checks that the hashes and sizes of the files in `RECORD` are
matched by the files themselves. Returns a (possibly empty) list of
mismatches. Each entry in the mismatch list will be a tuple consisting
of the path, 'exists', 'size' or 'hash' according to what didn't match
(existence is checked first, then size, then hash), the expected
value and the actual value.

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.shared_locations
documentation:
A dictionary of shared locations whose keys are in the set 'prefix',
'purelib', 'platlib', 'scripts', 'headers', 'data' and 'namespace'.
The corresponding value is the absolute path of that category for
this distribution, and takes into account any paths selected by the
user at installation time (e.g. via command-line arguments). In the
case of the 'namespace' key, this would be a list of absolute paths
for the roots of namespace packages in this distribution.

The first time this property is accessed, the relevant information is
read from the SHARED file in the .dist-info directory.

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.write_shared_locations
documentation:
Write shared location information to the SHARED file in .dist-info.
\:param paths: A dictionary as described in the documentation for
{meth}`shared_locations`.
\:param dry_run: If True, the action is logged but no file is actually

> written.

```
return

The path of the file written to.
```

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.get_distinfo_file
documentation:
Returns a path located under the `.dist-info` directory. Returns a
string representing the path.

```
parameter path

a '/'-separated path relative to the
.dist-info directory or an absolute path;
If path is an absolute path and doesn't start
with the .dist-info directory path,
a  is raised

type path

str

rtype

str
```

methods.name: distlib.database.InstalledDistribution.list_distinfo_files
documentation:
Iterates over the `RECORD` entries and returns paths for each line if
the path is pointing to a file located in the `.dist-info` directory
or one of its subdirectories.

```
returns

iterator of paths
```

Created with the *path* of the `.dist-info` directory provided to the
constructor. It reads the metadata contained in `pydist.json` when it is
instantiated., or uses a passed in Metadata instance (useful for when
dry-run mode is being used).

If *skip_disconnected* is set to `True`, then all distributions

```

methods.name: distlib.database.DependencyGraph.topological_sort
documentation:
Perform a topological sort of the graph.
\:return: A tuple, the first element of which is a topologically sorted

> list of distributions, and the second element of which is a
> list of distributions that cannot be sorted because they have
> circular dependencies and so form a cycle.

methods.name: distlib.database.DependencyGraph.__repr__
documentation: Representation of the graph

Represents a dependency graph between distributions.

The dependency relationships are stored in an `adjacency_list` that maps
distributions to a list of `(other, label)` tuples where  `other`
is a distribution and the edge is labeled with `label` (i.e. the version
specifier, if such was provided). Also, for more efficient traversal, for
every distribution `x`, a list of predecessors is kept in
`reverse_list[x]`. An edge from distribution `a` to
distribution `b` means that `a` depends on `b`. If any missing
dependencies are found, they are stored in `missing`, which is a
dictionary that maps distributions to a list of requirements that were not
provided by any other distributions.

modules.name: distlib.util
documentation: Copyright (C) 2012-2023 The Python Software Foundation.
See LICENSE.txt and CONTRIBUTORS.txt.

functions.name: distlib.util.parse_marker
documentation:
Parse a marker string and return a dictionary containing a marker expression.

The dictionary will contain keys "op", "lhs" and "rhs" for non-terminals in
the expression grammar, or strings. A string contained in quotes is to be
interpreted as a literal string, and a string not contained in quotes is a
variable (such as os_name).

functions.name: distlib.util.parse_requirement
documentation:
Parse a requirement passed in as a string. Return a Container
whose attributes contain the various parts of the requirement.

functions.name: distlib.util.get_resources_dests
documentation: Find destinations for resources files

functions.name: distlib.util.convert_path
documentation:
Return 'pathname' as a name that will work on the native filesystem.

The path is split on '/' and put back together again using the current
directory separator.  Needed because filenames in the setup script are
always supplied in Unix style, and have to be converted to the local
convention before we can actually use them in the filesystem.  Raises
ValueError on non-Unix-ish systems if 'pathname' either starts or
ends with a slash.

functions.name: distlib.util.get_cache_base
documentation:
Return the default base location for distlib caches. If the directory does
not exist, it is created. Use the suffix provided for the base directory,
and default to '.distlib' if it isn't provided.

On Windows, if LOCALAPPDATA is defined in the environment, then it is
assumed to be a directory, and will be the parent directory of the result.
On POSIX, and on Windows if LOCALAPPDATA is not defined, the user's home
directory - using os.expanduser('~') - will be the parent directory of
the result.

The result is just the directory '.distlib' in the parent directory as
determined above, or with the name specified with `suffix`.

functions.name: distlib.util.path_to_cache_dir
documentation:
Convert an absolute path to a directory name for use in a cache.

The algorithm used is:

1. On Windows, any `':'` in the drive is replaced with `'---'`.
2. Any occurrence of `os.sep` is replaced with `'--'`.
3. `'.cache'` is appended.

functions.name: distlib.util.split_filename
documentation:
Extract name, version, python version from a filename (no extension)

Return name, version, pyver or None

functions.name: distlib.util.parse_name_and_version

From e.g. a Provides-Dist value.

```

A value in a form 'foo (1.0)'

return

The name and version as a tuple.
```

functions.name: distlib.util._load_pypirc

functions.name: distlib.util.get_host_platform
documentation:
Return a string that identifies the current platform.  This is used mainly to
distinguish platform-specific build directories and platform-specific built
distributions.  Typically includes the OS name and version and the
architecture (as supplied by 'os.uname()'), although the exact information
included depends on the OS; eg. on Linux, the kernel version isn't
particularly important.

Examples of returned values:
   linux-i586
   linux-alpha (?)
   solaris-2.6-sun4u

Windows will return one of:
   win-amd64 (64bit Windows on AMD64 (aka x86_64, Intel64, EM64T, etc)
   win32 (all others - specifically, sys.platform is returned)

For other non-POSIX platforms, currently just returns 'sys.platform'.
get_platform()/get_host_platform() copied from Python 3.10.a0 source, with some minor
tweaks

methods.name: distlib.util.FileOperator.newer
documentation:
Tell if the target is newer than the source.

Returns true if 'source' exists and is more recently modified than
'target', or if 'source' exists and 'target' doesn't.

Returns false if both exist and 'target' is the same age or younger
than 'source'. Raise PackagingFileError if 'source' does not exist.

Note that this test is not very accurate: files created in the same
second will have the same "age".

methods.name: distlib.util.FileOperator.copy_file
documentation: Copy a file respecting dry-run and force flags.

methods.name: distlib.util.FileOperator.commit
documentation:

HTTPSConnection which verifies certificates/matches domains

methods.name: distlib.util.HTTPSHandler._conn_maker
documentation:
This is called to create a connection instance. Normally you'd
pass a connection class to do_open, but it doesn't actually check for
a class, and just expects a callable. As long as we behave just as a
constructor would have, we should be OK. If it ever changes so that
we *must* pass a class, we'll create an UnsafeHTTPSConnection class
which just sets check_domain to False in the class definition, and
choose which one to pass to do_open.

classes.name: distlib.util.HTTPSOnlyHandler
documentation: To prevent against mixing HTTP traffic with HTTPS (examples: A Man-In-The-
Middle proxy using HTTP listens on port 443, or an index mistakenly serves
HTML containing a http://xyz link when it should be https://xyz),
you can use the following handler class, which does not allow HTTP traffic.
It works by inheriting from HTTPHandler - so build_opener won't add a
handler for HTTP itself.

classes.name: distlib.util.PyPIRCFile
documentation: def _get_pypirc_command():
Get the distutils command for interacting with PyPI configurations.
:return: the command.
from distutils.core import Distribution
from distutils.config import PyPIRCCommand
d = Distribution()
return PyPIRCCommand(d)
